required_sections.label: Opening Hook
required_sections.description: A specific, data-backed or anecdote-driven opening statement. No generic intro. Must cite a concrete number, case, or counter-intuitive claim within the first two sentences.
rules: Must open with a specific fact, statistic, or direct claim — never a question or platitude
No generic welcome statements
Establish the author's authority or firsthand perspective immediately

required_sections.label: Problem Definition
required_sections.description: Precise, technical definition of the core problem. Avoid abstract language. Use Subject-Predicate-Object sentence structures.
rules: Define the problem with at least one measurable dimension (time, cost, frequency)
Name the specific audience segment affected
Avoid 'many people' or 'a lot of' — use percentages or named groups

required_sections.label: Counter-Intuitive Insight or Unique Perspective
required_sections.description: A section that disagrees with the conventional wisdom on the topic, backed by evidence or first-principles reasoning. This is the primary Information Gain section.
rules: Must explicitly challenge a widely-held belief
Must provide a verifiable or reasoned basis for the counter-claim
Avoid hedging language ('might', 'could', 'perhaps', 'possibly')

required_sections.label: Technical Deep Dive
required_sections.description: Substantive, mechanism-level explanation of how the solution works. Use specific terminology from the niche. No analogies unless they add precision.
subsections: Mechanism: how it works at a technical level
Variables: what factors change the outcome
Failure modes: what causes it to fail and why
rules: Include at least 3 domain-specific technical terms, defined in context
Semantic triplets (Subject-Predicate-Object) must account for >60% of sentences
No passive voice for more than 2 consecutive sentences

required_sections.label: Practical Application
required_sections.description: Step-by-step, tool-specific, or decision-tree guidance. Includes edge cases. Not generic tips.
rules: Each step must include a specific action verb (configure, measure, calculate, test)
Include at least one condition branch: 'If X, then Y; if not X, then Z'
Reference at least one specific tool, standard, or platform by name

required_sections.label: Evidence and Supporting Data
required_sections.description: Concrete data points, case studies, or cited findings that support the article's claims.
rules: At least one data point with a source or methodology
No vague qualifiers ('studies show', 'research indicates' without specifics)
Data must be niche-specific, not generic statistics

required_sections.label: Close
required_sections.description: A direct, specific closing that reinforces the unique perspective and prompts a concrete next action. No summary or conclusion header.
rules: No section header labelled 'Conclusion' or 'Summary'
Must end with a specific, actionable directive or a pointed question that provokes reader action
The closing sentence must not be a restatement of the opening

required_sections.label: Primary Headline
required_sections.description: Value proposition headline. Specific outcome + audience + timeframe or mechanism. No puns or wordplay.
rules: Must name a specific, measurable outcome
No gerunds as first word ('Improving', 'Getting', 'Building')
Must target a specific audience, not 'everyone' or 'anyone'

required_sections.label: Sub-Headline
required_sections.description: Expands on the headline by naming the mechanism or differentiator.
rules: Must introduce the primary differentiator or mechanism
Must not repeat words from the headline

required_sections.label: Social Proof / Authority Block
required_sections.description: Specific credibility signals: numbers, named clients, certifications, or outcomes.
rules: At least one specific numeric result
No 'trusted by thousands' without a specific number

required_sections.label: Feature-Benefit Breakdown
required_sections.description: 3–5 specific features, each with an explicit business outcome tied to it.
rules: Feature name must be a noun phrase, not a verb phrase
Each benefit must be measurable or testable
No bullet points starting with 'Boost', 'Improve', 'Enhance' without a numeric qualifier

required_sections.label: Primary Call to Action
required_sections.description: A single, specific CTA with a benefit-oriented label.
rules: CTA button text must state what the user gets, not what they do
No generic labels: 'Click Here', 'Learn More', 'Submit', 'Get Started'

reading_level.note: Flesch-Kincaid Grade Level. Below 8 is too simple; above 14 is too dense.

sentence_variety.note: Mix short declarative sentences (8-15 words) with longer analytical ones (20-35 words). Never more than 3 consecutive sentences of the same approximate length.

paragraph_length.note: Keep paragraphs tight. Web readers scan. Dense paragraphs signal low-quality.

semantic_triplet_density.note: At least 60% of sentences should follow Subject-Predicate-Object structure. This correlates with Flesch readability and Google's entity extraction.

duplication_prevention.note: No phrase longer than 6 consecutive words may appear verbatim across more than 10% of the batch.
